headline.purpose: The page title, e.g. 'FanGuard checkout'. One per screen.

body.purpose: Default copy and helper text. Cap prose at 65-75ch.

label.purpose: Form field labels and eyebrows (uppercase, 0.025em). Form labels only - not decoration.

shadows.name: button-xs
shadows.purpose: The only shadow in the system (Tailwind shadow-xs), on buttons. State-only; being retired in favor of tonal layering. Never paired with a border.

motion.name: ease-standard
motion.purpose: Default state transition (~150ms): hover, focus, color changes.

motion.name: press
motion.purpose: Tactile press feedback on buttons. Must have a prefers-reduced-motion: reduce fallback (no transform).

components.name: Primary Button
components.description: The single primary action per screen (e.g. 'Pay $5'). Ink on paper - in a monochrome system the CTA is maximum contrast.

components.name: Outline Button
components.description: Secondary action (e.g. 'Try again'). Paper with a hairline; tints on hover.

components.name: Checkout Card
components.description: The primary container - one decision per card. Shows the dollars-are-the-headline rule: the amount outweighs its label. (Border is current reality; target is a tonal surface step.)
components.html:
Tonight's premium$5

Pays out if Uruguay gets blown out.

components.name: Ticket Price Field
components.description: Money input with an inset $ prefix and a focus ring on the wrapper. Border is the one legitimate place lines stay.

components.name: Pay-with Select
components.description: Source-token picker. Same field vocabulary as the text input.

components.name: Amount Display
components.description: Signature money treatment - the loudest element on screen. tabular-nums, heaviest weight; the protected amount anchors against the loss.
components.html: Protect your night$250Premium $5

components.name: Flow Stepper
components.description: Three-step checkout spine: Cover priced -> Premium paid -> Cover secured. Active is Ink, done is emerald with a check. Plain-language, dollars-free, no jargon.
components.html:
1. ✓Cover priced
  2. 2Premium paid
  3. 3Cover secured

narrative.overview: FanGuard is a receipt you actually trust. A fan who just spent $250 on tickets has 10 seconds and zero patience for jargon; the interface answers one question - am I protected, and for how much? - the way a good receipt does: plain, legible, nothing to decode. The warmth in 'warm, human, fan-energy' is carried by clarity and plain language, not by ornament. The system is intentionally restrained: a near-monochrome ink-on-paper base where the money is the only thing that shouts. Dollar amounts are the largest, heaviest, most contrast-rich elements on every screen; everything else recedes. Density is low - one decision per screen, a single primary action. This restraint is the point, not a placeholder: a calm receipt has no business being colorful.
keyCharacteristics: Ink-on-paper restraint; money is the only loud element.
Dollars only - never a percentage or an odd, ever.
One screen, one decision, one primary action.
Plain language over jargon; 'your wallet' and '$', never 0xDd...F8D2 or 'USDC contract'.
Warmth comes from copy + a single accent, not from decoration.

rules.name: The Receipt Rule
rules.body: The surface is ink-on-paper. Color appears only to mean something - a payout (success), a failure (error) - never to fill space or add flavor. If a color isn't carrying meaning, it's wrong.

rules.name: The Color Debt Rule
rules.body: The system owes exactly one thing: a single warm brand accent for the primary 'protect'/'covered' moment. Until it lands, primary actions ride on Ink. Resolve it deliberately - do not sprinkle multiple new colors; the Receipt Rule still governs.

rules.name: The Dollars-Are-The-Headline Rule
rules.body: Money always uses tabular-nums and always out-weighs the copy around it. A premium or payout is the loudest thing in its card. Percentages and odds are forbidden on fan-facing screens - spell the trigger in plain English instead.

rules.name: The No-Border Rule
rules.body: Don't reach for a border to separate two surfaces - step the tint instead. A border is a last resort (e.g. an input's editable affordance), never the default way to define a card.

rules.name: The Flat-By-Default Rule
rules.body: Surfaces are flat at rest. Any shadow is a response to state (hover, press, a lifted popover), never ambient decoration - and never paired with a border on the same element.

dos: Do make the dollar amount the loudest element in any card - tabular-nums, heaviest weight, highest contrast.
Do speak plainly: 'your wallet', '$5', 'if Saudi Arabia wins big'. Money, not mechanics.
Do give every interactive control all its states - default, hover, focus-visible (3px ring), active/press, disabled, loading, error.
Do convey depth with tonal surface steps; reserve borders for input affordances.
Do keep cards at <=16px radius and <=1.25rem padding; one decision per screen.
Do provide a prefers-reduced-motion alternative for every transition, including the press-scale.
Do resolve the single warm accent deliberately before adding any other color.
donts: Don't show a percentage, a probability, or betting odds to the fan - ever.
Don't drift toward the gambling / sportsbook look: no neon, no live-odds tickers, no parlay-slip hype.
Don't drift toward crypto / web3 degen: no price charts, no gas/network jargon, no truncated wallet address as hero content, no dark-mode-neon.
Don't ship the generic SaaS dashboard: no sterile templated cards-everywhere, no gray-on-gray, no big-metric hero template.
Don't read as heavy insurance / legalese: no dense forms, fine print, or cold institutional blue.
Don't use a border-left/border-right > 1px as a colored accent stripe.
Don't use gradient text; emphasize with weight and size.
Don't pair a 1px border with a wide soft drop shadow on the same card or button.
Don't round cards past 16px or nest a card inside a card.
